text.title: ia-framework Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for ia-framework as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Org chart navigation — using internal reporting structure (Product, Engineering, Sales, Marketing sections) instead of user mental models (Features, Documentation, Community, Support).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Org-chart navigation mirrors how the company is structured, not how users think about the product. Users do not know which internal team owns a feature; they search by feature or task.

failure-mode.description: Too deep hierarchy — more than 3-4 levels in navigation.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Beyond 3 levels, users lose context of where they are and retreat to search. Each level adds a decision point and a chance for wrong turns.

failure-mode.description: Too many choices — more than 7 items per navigation level (Miller's Law).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Humans cannot comfortably hold more than 7 items in working memory while making a choice. Above 7 items, navigation becomes a search task in itself.

failure-mode.description: Jargon labels — using internal terminology (API Keys, Service Tiers, Webhook Integrations) in public navigation without translation.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Users working with the product understand jargon through repeated exposure, but first-time visitors and occasional users will not recognize terms. Test labels with people outside your company.

failure-mode.description: No testing — assumptions about navigation not validated with users.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Even experienced designers misjudge label clarity and category groupings. Card sorting and tree testing reveal mismatches; assumptions should not drive IA.

failure-mode.description: IA designed without user input reflects the organization's mental model, not users' — org-chart navigation is the most common failure.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Internal teams lose perspective on their own jargon. Org-chart navigation matches internal reporting structure, not user mental models of the product. Users do not think "engineering," "product," "sales" — they think about their jobs and the tools that solve them.

failure-mode.description: Tree testing validates findability of a specific label structure but not the navigation experience (visual design, microcopy, discoverability).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Tree testing strips visual design, making all navigation equally visible. In practice, visual design, color, proximity, and font size affect findability dramatically. A label that scores well in tree testing may still fail in visual UI if it is de-emphasized or buried.

failure-mode.description: Deep hierarchies (5+ levels) test well in tree testing but fail in practice because users lose context of where they are.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Tree testing asks discrete questions ("find this item") without context. Real navigation requires users to hold their location in the hierarchy in memory. Five levels exceeds working memory; users cannot remember where they are and give up.
